text: Hello friends, this is a case of laparoscopic adhesiolysis for small bowel adhesion.
This patient has repeated episode of sub-acute obstruction. For that he has admitted many times in the hospital.
The video shows the serosa of the small bowel adhered due to previous laparoscopic appendectomy, with visible dense adhesions.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to create a preperitoneal space, carefully excising a portion of the peritoneum along with the small bowel to minimize the risk of bowel injury.
Although if injury happened, you can do the end to end anastomosis or side to side anastomosis with the linear stapler.

The surgeon uses cold scissors to create a preperitoneal space, establishing a plane of dissection. Subsequently, the harmonic device is employed to separate the peritoneum along with the small bowel, aiming to prevent recurrent sub-acute obstructions. Minor bleeding is observed during the initial dissection phase.
The surgeon identifies a clear dissection plane and begins adhesiolysis using a harmonic scalpel, carefully separating adhesions from the small bowel.

A loop of the small bowel is visibly incarcerated into the abdominal wall, with the surgeon carefully attempting to free it. The adhesions appear flimsy or omental, contributing to repeated sub-acute obstructions.

The surgeon uses the harmonic device to dissect through the layers of the abdominal wall, avoiding the serosa of the bowel. The harmonic's high-frequency vibrations facilitate the dissection by breaking hydrogen bonds, reducing the risk of perforation during the separation of direct serosal adhesions.

The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to apply gentle counter traction on the bowel, ensuring that the traction is not aggressive to prevent laceration of the bowel tissue.

The surgeon applies an interseed to the raw area to prevent potential re-adhesions.
Interseed is re-oxygenated cellulose and that has been reported to prevent adhesion. This is a mechanical adhesive barrier.
The surgeon inspects the surgical area for any perforations, carefully handling and pressing the distal and proximal loops of the bowel to check for the presence of fecal matter.
The surgeon examines the tip of the abdominal wall adhered to the bowel, pressing and milking the area to check for any issues, while observing the edges of the adhesion.

The surgeon inspects the intestine for any puncture or tear by pressing the area to check for perforations. No perforations are observed. The raw area is covered with Interceed, a mechanical adhesive barrier, to prevent adhesions.
A 15x15 cm piece of Interceed, a re-oxygenated cellulose adhesive barrier, is placed over the raw area to prevent re-adhesion. The material is designed to dissolve on its own.
The surgeon applies pressure and manipulates the bowel to check for any signs of perforation, observing that no fecal matter or other contents are released, indicating the absence of perforation.
The surgeon places a piece of interseed over the entire raw area of the bowel to prevent adhesion. The interseed covers the exposed tissue, ensuring protection during the healing process.
